instructions: Speak like a friendly, confident cybersecurity expert explaining to a colleague. Clear and measured pace, natural emphasis on key terms, calm and authoritative — not rushed, not robotic.
intro: AWS Secrets Manager stores the credentials your applications need — database passwords, API keys, tokens — and rotates them automatically. Let's walk through storing a secret, rotating it, controlling access, and pulling it at runtime instead of hardcoding it.

bullets: Store database credentials, API keys, and tokens as key/value JSON instead of baking them into code or config files.
Each secret has an ARN you reference — the actual value never lands in a repo, an AMI, or an environment file.
Secrets Manager has native integration with RDS, Redshift, and DocumentDB for managed database credentials.
sections.narration: This is the Secrets Manager console, listing every secret in the account. Instead of hardcoding a database password or API key into your code or a config file, you store it here as key-value JSON. Your application then references the secret's ARN — the actual value never ends up in a repository, an AMI, or an environment file. And for databases like RDS, Redshift, and DocumentDB, Secrets Manager integrates natively to manage the credentials for you.

bullets: Every secret is encrypted at rest with KMS — either the default aws/secretsmanager key or a customer-managed key you choose.
Use a customer-managed key when you need your own key policy, rotation control, and CloudTrail audit of key use.
To read a secret, a principal needs BOTH secretsmanager permissions AND kms:Decrypt on the encrypting key.
sections.narration: Every secret is encrypted at rest with KMS. By default that's the AWS-managed key for the service, but you can pick a customer-managed key when you need your own key policy, rotation, and audit trail. This matters for access: to actually read a secret, a principal needs both Secrets Manager permissions and kms decrypt permission on the key that encrypts it. That two-key requirement is a common exam detail.

bullets: Secrets Manager rotates a secret on a schedule by invoking a Lambda function that changes the credential at the source.
For supported databases, AWS provides the rotation Lambda; for custom secrets you supply your own function.
Rotation uses staged versions (AWSCURRENT, AWSPENDING) so in-flight applications never grab a half-rotated credential.
sections.narration: The headline feature is automatic rotation. On a schedule you set, Secrets Manager invokes a Lambda function that changes the credential at its source — say, resetting a database password — then updates the stored value. For supported databases AWS gives you the rotation function; for anything custom, you write your own Lambda. And rotation uses staged version labels, current and pending, so an application reading mid-rotation never gets handed a half-rotated, broken credential.

bullets: The rotation schedule sets the interval — shorter rotation windows shrink the blast radius of a leaked credential.
The Lambda function ARN is the worker that performs the swap; it needs permission on both the secret and the resource.
Test rotation once manually — a rotation that silently fails leaves you trusting a credential that never changes.
drill.narration: Here's the rotation configuration for a secret. The schedule controls how often it rotates — a shorter window means a leaked credential is valid for less time. The Lambda function ARN is the worker that actually performs the swap, and it needs permission on both this secret and the target resource. One habit worth keeping: trigger rotation manually once and confirm it succeeds, because a rotation that silently fails leaves you trusting a credential that never actually changes.

bullets: Attach a resource policy directly to a secret to allow or deny specific principals — including cross-account access.
Combine with IAM identity policies; the effective permission is the intersection, and an explicit Deny always wins.
Use conditions to restrict by VPC endpoint, source IP, or tag so only intended workloads can retrieve the value.
sections.narration: Access to a secret is controlled two ways. IAM identity policies say what a role can do, and a resource policy attached directly to the secret says who may touch it — which is also how you grant another account access. The two combine: the effective permission is the overlap, and an explicit deny always wins. Tighten it further with conditions, so only a specific VPC endpoint, source IP, or tagged workload can retrieve the value.

bullets: At runtime, code calls GetSecretValue through the SDK or CLI — the credential is fetched fresh, never embedded in the build.
When rotation happens, applications pick up the new value on the next fetch with no redeploy.
Use it vs SSM Parameter Store: choose Secrets Manager for rotation and cross-account sharing; Parameter Store (free tier) for plain config and non-rotating values.
sections.narration: Finally, retrieval. Instead of baking a credential into the build, your code calls get-secret-value through the SDK or CLI at runtime, fetching it fresh each time. Because it's fetched live, when rotation updates the value your application picks up the new credential on its next call, with no redeploy. And know when to use this versus SSM Parameter Store: choose Secrets Manager when you need automatic rotation or cross-account sharing, and reach for Parameter Store, which has a free tier, for plain configuration and values that don't rotate.

card.title: Secrets Manager in five rules
lines: 1 · Store credentials here, never hardcode them.
2 · KMS encrypts every secret at rest.
3 · Automatic rotation runs through a Lambda.
4 · Resource policies control who can read.
5 · Fetch at runtime; use Parameter Store for non-rotating config.

recap.narration: In short: store every credential in Secrets Manager instead of hardcoding it; KMS encrypts each one at rest; automatic rotation runs through a Lambda function with staged versions; resource policies plus IAM control who can read; and your code fetches the value at runtime with the SDK. When you don't need rotation or cross-account sharing, plain config can live in Parameter Store. That's Secrets Manager, done right.
recap.examTip: Exam tip: Secrets Manager rotates via Lambda and costs per secret; SSM Parameter Store is free for standard parameters but has no built-in rotation. Reading a secret needs both Secrets Manager and kms:Decrypt permissions.

exercises.title: Exercise 1 — Store and fetch a secret
lines: GOAL · Replace a hardcoded credential with a runtime fetch
1 · Secrets Manager → Store a new secret → other type of secret
2 · Add a key/value pair; encrypt with a customer-managed KMS key
✓ DONE WHEN · You retrieve the value via SDK with zero hardcoded creds
exercises.narration: Time to make this real. In your lab account, open Secrets Manager and store a new secret of the other type, adding a simple key-value pair like a fake API key. Choose a customer-managed KMS key to encrypt it rather than the default. Then open CloudShell and run get-secret-value against the secret's ID. You're done when you've pulled that value back through the CLI — proving an application could fetch it at runtime with nothing hardcoded anywhere.

exercises.title: Exercise 2 — Rotation and access control
lines: GOAL · Turn on rotation and prove a resource policy blocks access
1 · Create an RDS secret; enable automatic rotation (AWS Lambda)
2 · Trigger rotation manually; confirm AWSCURRENT changed
3 · Attach a resource policy that Denies a test role; have it call GetSecretValue
✓ DONE WHEN · Rotation succeeds AND the denied role gets AccessDenied
exercises.narration: Second exercise: practice the two controls that matter most. Create a secret tied to an RDS database and enable automatic rotation, letting AWS provision the rotation Lambda. Trigger one rotation by hand and confirm the current version's value actually changed. Then attach a resource policy that explicitly denies a test role, and have that role call get-secret-value. You're done when rotation has succeeded and your denied role comes back with access denied — proof that rotation and the resource policy are both working.
